author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

ccel.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

title: Psalm XLIII

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This Psalm is a short one; it satisfies the mental cravings of the hearers, without imposing too severe a trial on the hunger of those fasting.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let our soul feed upon it; our soul, which he who sings in this Psalm, speaks of as “cast down;” cast down, I suppose, either in consequence of some fist, or rather in consequence of some hunger he was in.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For fasting is a voluntary act; being an-hungered is an involuntary thing.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That which is an-hungered, is the Church, is the Body of Christ: and that “Man” who is extended throughout the whole world, of which the Head is above, the limbs below: it is His voice which ought by this time to be perfectly known, and perfectly familiar, to us, in all the Psalms; now chanting joyously, now sorrowing; now rejoicing in hope, now sighing at its actual state, even as if it were our own.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We need not then dwell long on pointing out to you, who is the speaker here: let each one of us be a member of Christ’s Body; and he will be speaker here.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Judge me, O Lord, and separate my cause from the ungodly nation” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I do not dread Thy judgment, because I know Thy mercy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Judge me, O God,” he cries.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now, meanwhile, in this state of pilgrimage, Thou dost not yet separate my place, because I am to live together with the “tares” even to the time of the “harvest:” Thou dost not as yet separate my rain from theirs; my light from theirs: “separate my cause.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let a difference be made between him who believes in Thee, and him who believes not in Thee.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Our infirmity is the same; but our consciences not the same: our sufferings the same; but our longings not the same.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The desire of the ungodly shall perish,” but as to the desire of the righteous, we might well doubt, if He were not “sure” who promised.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The object of our desires is He Himself, who promiseth: He will give us Himself, because He has already given Himself to us; He will give Himself in His immortality to us then immortal, even because He gave Himself in His mortality to us when mortal.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And since patience is needful in order to endure, until the harvest, a certain distinction without separation, if we may so speak (for they are together with us, and therefore not yet separated; the tares however being still tares, and the corn still corn, and therefore they are already distinct); since then a kind of strength is needful, which must be implored of Him who bids us to be strong, and without whose making us strong, we should not be what He bids us to be; of Him who said, “He that endures unto the end shall be saved,” lest the soul’s powers should be impaired in consequence of her ascribing any strength to herself, he subjoins immediately,

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For Thou, O God, art my strength: why hast Thou cast me off, and why go I mourning, while the enemy harasseth me?” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I go mourning: the enemy is harassing me with daily temptations: inspiring either some unlawful love, or some ungrounded cause of fear; and the soul that fights against both of them, though not taken prisoner by them, yet being in danger from them, is contracted with sorrow, and says unto God, “Why?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let her then ask of Him, and hear “Why?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For she is in the Psalm enquiring the cause of her dejection; saying, “Why hast Thou cast me off? and why go I mourning?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let her hear from Isaiah; let the lesson which has just been read, suggest itself to her.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The spirit shall go forth from me, and every breath have I made.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For iniquity have I a little afflicted him; I hid my face from him, and he departed from me sorrowful in the ways of his heart.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Why then didst thou ask, “Why hast Thou cast me off, and why go I mourning?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thou hast heard, it was “for iniquity.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Iniquity” is the cause of thy mourning; let “Righteousness” be the cause of thy rejoicing!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thou wouldest sin; and yet thou wouldest fain not suffer; so that it was too little for thee to be thyself unrighteous, without also wishing Him to be unrighteous, in that thou wouldest fain not be punished by Him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Consider a speech of a better kind in another Psalm.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “It is good for me that Thou hast humbled me, that I might learn Thy righteousnesses.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By being lifted up, I had learned my own iniquities; let me by being “humbled,” learn “Thy righteousnesses.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Why go I mourning, while the enemy harasses me?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thou complainest of the enemy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is true he does harass thee; but it was thou didst “give place” to him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And even now there is a course open to thee; choose the course of prudence; admit thy King, shut the tyrant out.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But in order that she may do this, hear what she says, what she supplicates, what she prays for.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Pray thou for what thou hearest; pray for it when thou hearest it; let these words be the voice of us all: “O send out Thy Light and Thy Truth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: They have led me, and brought me on unto Thy holy hill, and into Thy Tabernacles” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For that very “Light” and “Truth” are indeed two in name; the reality expressed is but One.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For what else is the “Light” of God, except the “Truth” of God?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or what else is the “Truth” of God, except the “Light” of God?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And the one Person of Christ is both of these.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I am the Light of the world: he that believeth on Me, shall not walk in darkness.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I am the Way, the Truth, and the Life.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He is Himself “the Light:” He is Himself “the Truth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let Him come then and rescue us, and “separate at once our cause from the ungodly nation; let Him deliver us from the deceitful and unjust man,” let him separate the wheat from the tares, for at the time of harvest He will Himself send His Angels, that they may “gather out of His kingdom all things that offend,” and cast them into flaming fire, while they gather together the corn into the garner.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He will send out His “Light,” and His “Truth;” for that they have already “brought us and led us to His holy hill, and into His Tabernacles.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We possess the “earnest;” we hope for the prize.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “His holy Hill” is His holy Church.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is that mountain which, according to Daniel’s vision, grew from a very small “stone,” till it crushed the kingdoms of the earth; and grew to such a size, that it “filled the face of the earth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is the “hill,” from which he tells us that his prayer was heard, who says, “I cried unto the Lord with my voice, and He heard me out of His holy hill.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let no one of those that are without that mountain, hope to be heard unto eternal life.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For many are heard in their prayers for many things.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let them not congratulate themselves on being heard; the devils were heard in their prayer, that they might be sent into the swine.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let us desire to be heard unto eternal life, by reason of our longing, through which we say, “Send out Thy Light and Thy Truth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That is a “Light” which requires the eye of the heart.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For “Blessed” (He saith) “are the pure in heart, for they shall see God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We are now on His Hill, that is, in His Church, and in His Tabernacle.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The “tabernacle” is for persons sojourning; the house, for those dwelling in one community.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The tabernacle is also for those who are both from home, and also in a state of warfare.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When thou hearest of a tabernacle, form a notion of a war; guard against an enemy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But what shall the house be?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Blessed are they that dwell in Thine house: they will be alway praising Thee.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now then that we have been led on even to “the Tabernacle,” and are placed on “His holy Hill,” what hope do we carry with us?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Then will I go in unto the Altar of God” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For there is a certain invisible Altar on high, which the unrighteous man approaches not.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To that Altar he alone draws nigh, who draws nigh to this one without cause to fear.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There he shall find his Life, who in this one “separates his cause.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And I will go in unto the Altar of God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: From His holy Hill, and from His Tabernacle, from His Holy Church, I will go in unto the Altar of God on High.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What manner of Sacrifice is there?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He himself who goeth in is taken for a burnt-offering.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I will go in unto the Altar of God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What is the meaning of what he says, “The Altar of my God”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Unto God, who makes glad my youth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Youth signifies newness: just as if he said, “Unto God, who makes glad my newness.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is He who makes glad my newness, who hath filled my old estate with mourning.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For now “I go mourning” in oldness, then shall “I stand,” exulting in newness!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Yea, upon the harp will I praise Thee, O God my God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What is the meaning of “praising on the harp,” and praising on the psaltery?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he does not always do so with the harp, nor always with the psaltery.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These two instruments of the musicians have each a distinct meaning of their own, worthy of our consideration and notice.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: They are both borne in the hands, and played by the touch; and they stand for certain bodily works of ours.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Both are good, if one knows how to play the psaltery, or to play the harp.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But since the psaltery is that instrument which has the shell (i.e. that drum, that hollow piece of wood, by straining on which the chords resound) on the upper part of it, whereas the harp has that same concave sounding-board on the lower part, there is to be a distinction made between our works, when they are “upon the harp,” when “on the psaltery:” both however are acceptable to God, and grateful to His ear.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When we do anything according to God’s Commandments, obeying His commands and hearkening to Him, that we may fulfil His injunctions, when we are active and not passive, it is the psaltery that is playing.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For so also do the Angels: for they have nothing to suffer.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But when we suffer anything of tribulation, of trials, of offences on this earth (as we suffer only from the inferior part of ourselves; i.e. from the fact that we are mortal, that we owe somewhat of tribulation to our original cause, and also from the fact of our suffering much from those who are not “above”); this is “the harp.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For there rises a sweet strain from that part of us which is “below:” we “suffer,” and we strike the psaltery, or shall I rather say we sing and we strike the harp.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And again, in order that he may draw the sound from that sounding-board below, he addresses his soul: he says, “Why art thou sorrowful, O my soul, and why dost thou disquiet me?” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I am in tribulations, in weariness, in mourning, “Why dost thou disquiet me, O my soul?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Who is the speaker, to whom is he speaking?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That it is the soul to which he is speaking, everybody knows: for it is obvious: the appeal is addressed to it directly: “Why art thou sorrowful, O my soul, and why dost thou disquiet me?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The question is as to the speaker.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is not the flesh addressing the soul, surely, since the flesh cannot speak without the soul.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is more appropriate for the soul to address the flesh, than for the flesh to address the soul.…We perceive then that we have a certain part, in which is “the image of God;” viz. the mind and reason.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It was that same mind that prayed for “God’s Light” and “God’s Truth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is the same mind by which we apprehend right and wrong: it is by the same that we discern truth from falsehood.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is this same that we call “understanding;” which “understanding,” indeed, is wanting to the brutes.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And this “understanding” whoever neglects in himself, and holds it in less account than the other parts of his nature, and casts it off, just as if he had it not, is addressed in the Psalm, “Be ye not as the horse and the mule, which have no understanding.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is our “understanding” then that is addressing our soul.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The latter is withered away from tribulations, worn out in anguish, made “sorrowful” in temptations, fainting in toils.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The mind, catching a glimpse of Truth above, would fain rouse her spirits, and she says, “Why art thou sorrowful, O my soul?”…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These expressions, brethren, are safe ones: but yet be watchful in good works.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Touch “the psaltery,” by obeying the Commandments; touch the harp, by patiently enduring your sufferings.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: You have heard from Isaiah, “Break thy bread to the hungry;” think not that fasting by itself is sufficient.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Fasting chasteneth thine own self: it does not refresh others.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thy distress will profit thee, if thou affordest comfort to others.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: See, thou hast denied thyself; to whom wilt thou give that of which thou hast deprived thyself?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Where wilt thou bestow what thou hast denied thyself?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: How many poor may be filled by the breakfast we have this day given up?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Fast in such a way that thou mayest rejoice, that thou hast breakfasted, while another has been eating; fast on account of thy prayers, that thou mayest be heard in them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For He says in that passage, “Whilst thou art yet speaking I will say, Here I am,” provided thou wilt with cheerful mind “break thy bread to the hungry.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For generally this is done by men reluctantly and with murmurs, to rid themselves of the wearisome importunity of the beggar, not to refresh the bowels of him that is needy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But it is “a cheerful giver” that “God loves.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If thou givest thy bread reluctantly, thou hast lost both the bread, and the merit of the action.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Do it then from the heart: that He “who seeth in secret,” may say, “whilst thou art yet speaking, Here I am.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: How speedily are the prayers of those received, who work righteousness!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And this is man’s righteousness in this life, fasting, alms, and prayer.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wouldest thou have thy prayer fly upward to God?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Make for it those two wings of alms and fasting.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Such may God’s “Light” and God’s “Truth” find us, that He may find us without cause for fear, when He comes to free us from death, who has already come to undergo death for us.
